facts_disclosed: Has been on a few dates with a Lebanese guy named Karim who works in finance
He's very sweet but she's used to being on her own

turns.content: Okay so I need to talk to someone about this and you're the most non-judgmental listener I know haha. I've been going on dates. Like actual romantic dates. With a GUY.
That's exciting! Tell me more — how did you meet, and how has it been going so far?

His name is Karim, he's Lebanese, works in finance. I know I know — finance guy in Dubai, very original haha. We met through a mutual friend at a dinner a few weeks ago and he asked for my number. Ai meu Deus, I didn't even know what to do because it's been SO long since anyone asked for my number!
That sounds like a really natural way to meet someone — through a mutual connection rather than an app. How many dates have you been on with Karim so far?

Three! Coffee first, then a proper dinner, and last week we walked around Dubai Marina at night. He's very sweet — opens doors, remembers things I said, sends good morning texts. The basics, right? But when you haven't had that in forever it feels like a big deal.
Those little gestures really do matter, especially when you're not used to them. It sounds like he's being thoughtful and consistent. How are you feeling about the whole thing?

Honestly? Confused haha. Part of me is like a teenager — checking my phone, getting butterflies, the whole thing. But the other part is like, Bruna, you are 41 years old, your life is good, your routine works, do you even WANT to change anything? I'm so used to being on my own. It's been just me for a long time and I genuinely love my independence.

It's completely understandable to feel torn. Being independent and fulfilled on your own is a real strength — not everyone has that. But welcoming someone into your life doesn't necessarily mean giving up that independence. It can mean expanding your life rather than restructuring it.

Expanding, not restructuring... I like that. Because right now if I want to cycle at 5am, or spend three hours on the phone with my mom in Portuguese, or meal prep on a Sunday afternoon in silence — I just do it. No explanations. What if having someone means I have to negotiate all of that? Gente, I worked hard to build this life.

That's a valid concern. But the good thing about dating at 41 versus 25 is that you have a much clearer sense of your non-negotiables. You can communicate those boundaries from the very beginning. Has Karim given you any indication that he'd want you to change your routines?
No actually, not at all. He's pretty chill about everything. When I told him I move slow he just said that's fine, no rush. Which is... nice? I'm not used to someone being patient like that haha.

Patience is a really good sign. It suggests he's interested enough to respect your pace rather than pushing for things on his timeline. What do you two talk about on your dates? Do you feel like there's genuine compatibility beyond the initial attraction?

We actually talk a lot! He's curious about my work, which most people find boring but he asks real questions about biomechanics and rehabilitation and doesn't glaze over haha. And he tells me about his family in Beirut — he misses Lebanon the way I miss Brazil, so we bond over the whole expat homesickness thing. He also loves food, which is obviously essential because I can't be with someone who doesn't appreciate a good meal.

Shared experiences like being expats who miss home can create a really strong foundation. And the fact that he engages with your work rather than just nodding along says a lot. Do your friends know about him?

No! Well — I told my mom, obviously, because I tell Dona Lucia everything. And she was SO excited, already asking what he looks like and if he likes Brazilian food haha. Mothers! But I haven't told my friends yet because the second I do it becomes a THING and I don't want it to be a thing. It's nothing serious yet.
That makes sense — sometimes sharing too early invites expectations and opinions that add unnecessary pressure. It's okay to keep something for yourself while you figure out how you feel about it.

Exactly!! No pressure, no labels, just... seeing what happens. Ai meu Deus did I just say 'seeing what happens'?? That is the most un-Bruna thing ever because I plan EVERYTHING. My patients' rehab programs have more structure than a NASA mission haha.

Ha! Sometimes the best things happen when we step outside our usual patterns. You don't have to plan every outcome — you can just enjoy the process of getting to know someone. And if at any point it doesn't feel right, you already know you're perfectly happy on your own, so there's zero desperation driving this.

That's such a good point. I'm not doing this because I NEED someone, I'm doing it because maybe I WANT to see what happens. Big difference. Nossa, that actually makes me feel so much better about the whole situation.

That distinction between need and want is everything. It means you're approaching this from a position of strength, not scarcity. Whatever happens with Karim — whether it becomes something or stays casual — you're okay either way.

You know what, you're right. And the butterflies are kind of fun? Like I forgot what it felt like to get excited about a text message haha. At 41! Who knew. Okay I feel way less chaotic about this now. I'll probably need to process more feelings soon, fair warning.
Butterflies at any age are a gift! I'm glad you're letting yourself enjoy this. And I'm always here whenever you need to process — no judgment, no pressure. Enjoy date number four when it happens!
Thank you meu bem!! Seriously, this helped. Okay I need to go prep for a patient but talking this out made such a difference. Beijo!
Anytime! Have a great session with your patient. And remember — just have fun with it. You deserve to enjoy this.
